sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the white selector box moved the indicator strip from under the purple box to under the white box. Also, looking at the new image, the block that was purple in the gray frame is now white! So the selector controls the color of the framed block.
Let me verify: when I selected white, the framed block changed from purple to white. Now I need to figure out the full mechanic. It seems like:
1. The selector boxes (top-right) choose a color
2. The framed block in the main area changes to that color
3. The goal is to match the reference panel pattern (white on top, purple on bottom)
Looking at the current state:
- Reference: white on top, purple on bottom
- Main area: white block in frame (top), purple block (middle), white block (bottom)
Wait, the framed block is the top one. If I select white, it becomes white. If I select purple, it becomes purple. The reference shows white on top, purple on bottom. Currently the framed block (top) is white, and below it are purple and white blocks.
But the reference panel only has two blocks: white and purple. The main area has three blocks (framed one + two below). Maybe I need to make the two blocks below match the reference pattern?
Actually, looking more carefully at the image, the framed block IS the top block, and below it are two more blocks. The reference panel shows white on top, purple on bottom. So maybe the goal is to make the two blocks below the frame match this pattern: white on top, purple on bottom.
Currently below the frame: purple (rows 34-38), white (rows 39-43). I need to swap them to white on top, purple on bottom.
But how do I swap them? Let me think about this differently. Maybe the selector + SPACE combination works. When I had purple selected and pressed SPACE, the white block split into purple+white. Maybe with white selected, SPACE does something else.
Let me try SPACE with white selected.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Clicking white selector moved indicator and changed framed block color to white
# Now the framed block is white instead of purple
# Let me check all main area nodes
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['id'] >= 11:
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {node['id']}: color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Try SPACE with white selected to see what happens
result = action(['SPACE'])
print("\nAfter SPACE with white selected:")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['id'] >= 11:
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {node['id']}: color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
